question: A running process instance must react automatically when one of its own process variables changes value, with no external system pushing a message. What does the routing best-practice say about doing this in Camunda 8?

options.text: Camunda 8 does not yet support the BPMN conditional event, so a different mechanism (e.g., evaluate the condition externally and publish a message) is required.
Use a non-interrupting boundary timer with cycle `R/PT0S` to re-evaluate the variable on every change.
Add an event subprocess with an interrupting signal start event named after the variable.
Use an intermediate conditional event — Camunda 8 evaluates the condition on every variable write.

a.text: The 'reacting to process-internal events' section carries a caution: Camunda 8 does not yet support the conditional event. The recommended workaround is to evaluate the condition externally and route the result back via a message.

b.text: Timer cycles fire on time, not on variable writes; a timer cannot act as a variable-change listener.

c.text: Signal events are broadcast triggers from outside the instance; they are not how an instance subscribes to its own variable changes.

d.text: Conditional events are exactly the construct that Camunda 8 does not yet support; the docs warn against assuming the engine will re-evaluate on every variable write.

explanation: The loan-application example in the best-practice page describes a conditional boundary event watching a variable changed by a user task. The caution box right next to it states that Camunda 8 does not yet support the conditional event, so the supported workaround is to externalise the condition check and feed the result back to the process via a message.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices
